findings.title: Per-creator index / global swapId confusion lets callers withdraw other users' escrowed assets

best_agent_match.title: Global swap index mismatch lets attackers withdraw assets from other users' escrows

findings.title: User-controlled `typeStd` can route whitelisted assets through an unset custom bridge and bypass escrow entirely

best_agent_match.title: User-controlled `typeStd` can bypass escrow through the custom bridge path

findings.title: ERC20 transfers are treated as successful even when the token signals failure

best_agent_match.title: ERC20 transfers are treated as successful even when the token returns `false`

findings.title: `cancelSwapIntent` is reentrant through token or bridge callbacks before the swap is marked cancelled

best_agent_match.title: Reentrancy in `cancelSwapIntent` can pay out the same ETH escrow multiple times

findings.title: Using Solidity `transfer` for ETH payouts can permanently brick swaps involving contract recipients

best_agent_match.title: Using `transfer` for ETH payouts can permanently brick swaps for contract accounts

rejected_candidates.title: Reentrancy Vulnerability in ETH Transfers
rejected_candidates.reason: `closeSwapIntent` sets the swap status to `Closed` before any ETH payout, and Solidity `transfer` forwards only 2300 gas, so the claimed fallback-based reentrancy path is not viable.

rejected_candidates.title: Owner Can Steal All Assets via Address Changes
rejected_candidates.reason: This is an owner-trust/governance concern, not an unintended protocol bug. Changing `VAULT` redirects future fees, not existing user escrow, and malicious owner address changes are outside the normal threat model.

rejected_candidates.title: Swap Creator Can Edit Counterpart After Asset Deposit
rejected_candidates.reason: There is no prior counterparty deposit to steal. `nftsTwo` and `valueTwo` are pulled atomically from the caller during `closeSwapIntent`, so changing `addressTwo` does not confiscate already-escrowed assets from someone else.

rejected_candidates.title: PunkProxy Reinitialization Vulnerability
rejected_candidates.reason: This describes user key-management / recovery limitations, not an exploitable protocol flaw.

rejected_candidates.title: Owner Can Pause and Set Malicious Payment Configuration
rejected_candidates.reason: This is another owner-trust concern rather than an unintended permissionless exploit.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Expiration Check for Swaps
rejected_candidates.reason: `swapEnd` is used as a completion timestamp, not an expiry promise. Counterparty assets are not pre-deposited, so the described permanent lockup path is not supported by the code.

rejected_candidates.title: No Slippage Protection for NFT Swaps
rejected_candidates.reason: This is inherent market risk in fixed-term OTC swaps, not a smart-contract vulnerability.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing check for contract existence before external calls
rejected_candidates.reason: As written this mostly relies on admin miswhitelisting. The materially reportable user-triggerable version is the `typeStd` custom-bridge bypass captured in F-002.

rejected_candidates.title: Inconsistent Access Control on WhiteList Management
rejected_candidates.reason: The claim is factually incorrect: `setWhitelist(address,bool)` can both add and remove whitelist entries by setting the boolean status.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Event Emission for Critical State Changes
rejected_candidates.reason: Lack of events is a transparency issue, not a realistic protocol-level asset loss or lockup vulnerability.

rejected_candidates.title: Incorrect ERC20 Interface Definition
rejected_candidates.reason: The parameter name `tokenId` is irrelevant to the ABI; the function selector matches `transferFrom(address,address,uint256)`.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Contract Balance Recovery Function
rejected_candidates.reason: Accidental direct transfers to the contract are a usability issue, not an exploitable protocol vulnerability in the swap flow.
